IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON INDUSTRIAL ELECTRONICS, VOL. 58, NO. 5, MAY 2011
1625
A Failure-Detection Strategy for IGBT Based
on Gate-Voltage Behavior Applied to
a Motor Drive System
Marco Antonio Rodríguez-Blanco, Member, IEEE, Abraham Claudio-Sánchez, Member, IEEE,
Didier Theilliol, Luis Gerardo Vela-Valdés, Pedro Sibaja-Terán,
Leobardo Hernández-González, Member, IEEE, and Jesus Aguayo-Alquicira, Member, IEEE
Abstract—In this paper, a novel failure-detection technique and
its analog circuit for insulated gate bipolar transistors (IGBTs),
under open- and short-circuit failures, are proposed. This tech-
nique is applied to a three-phase induction-motor (IM) drive
system. The detection technique is adapted to detect failures of
short-circuit and open-circuit in the IGBT, which is based on
gate-signal monitoring. The most important issue of this technique
is the reduction of time for fault detection. This is very important
in a failure-tolerant IM drive based on the material-redundancy
approach or protection systems since the detection must be done
before the device is damaged, in approximately less than 10 μs.
The experimental test and simulations are presented in order to
valida

y
approach or protection systems since the detection must be done
before the device is damaged, in approximately less than 10 μs.
The experimental test and simulations are presented in order to
validate the proposed fault-detection technique, and it is validated,
achieving replacement of the damaged element in the most suitable
time.
Index Terms—Analog circuits, driver circuits, fault location,
insulated gate bipolar transistors (IGBTs), semiconductor-device
measurements, time delay.
NOMENCLATURE
AGD
Gate–drain area.
CGD
Gate–drain capacitance.
CGDJ
Gate–drain depletion capacitance.
COXD
Gate–drain oxide capacitance.
NB
n-layer doping concentration.
q
Electron charge.

I. INTRODUCTION
T
ECHNOLOGICAL advances have allowed very complex
industrial processes with high degree of automation. This
has improved the quality of its products and increased the
efﬁciency in its processes. Consequently, this makes a most
complex system and more vulnerability to faults; this is due
to the great exigency of automation and safety in modern
production systems. Thus, presently, there is a growing need
and interest in developing fault-tolerant systems, which contain
tolerant mechanisms that make more reliable equipment when
a fault occurs on the system [1]–[3]. This is feasible for the
case of study where control objectives allow a moderate de-
lay time in order to diagnose faults (detection, location, and
identiﬁcation), which generally are focused to incipient faults
of mechatronic systems. However, there are abrupt faults in
some subsystems where it is not possible to acc

ts (detection, location, and
identiﬁcation), which generally are focused to incipient faults
of mechatronic systems. However, there are abrupt faults in
some subsystems where it is not possible to accept a moderate
delay time. In this case, the time for diagnostic has to be as
fast as possible in order to avoid the propagation of fault,
e.g., in the switching of communication systems [3] or power-
electronic systems [4]. It is important to highlight that the fault-
detection stage is the one which takes up most of the delay
time because the ﬁrst physical contact when the fault is carried
out occurs at this phase. In [5], a failure-prognostic system for
insulated gate bipolar transistor (IGBT) has been developed
based on early symptoms and warning signs of power-module
degradation due to thermomechanical stress but using a special
degradation trace on the VCEsat signal, which is also used to
detect overcurrent in the IGBT. However, the platform used is
not in real time, and fault identiﬁcation has not been considered.
An interesting application, for the case of study, in switching
systems of power electronics is the induction-motor (IM) drive.
Generally, this kind of application is

ﬁcation has not been considered.
An interesting application, for the case of study, in switching
systems of power electronics is the induction-motor (IM) drive.
Generally, this kind of application is integrated to many critical
processes where the speed-tracking problem is an essential
issue to maintain the quality of products and reliability of the
process to avoid production cuts, such as in the metallurgical
and paper industries, among others, where there are many fault-
tolerant schemes applied to motor drive [6]–[13]. In [9], a
research is aimed on a new algorithm design for fault diagnostic
0278-0046/$26.00 © 2010 IEEE


[Page 2]
1626
IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON INDUSTRIAL ELECTRONICS, VOL. 58, NO. 5, MAY 2011
and fault reconﬁguration. Also, new tolerant-scheme analyses
of converters based on material redundancy of the inverter drive
with dual pack of IGBTs as redundant unit are presented in
[6]–[10], multilevel inverter drives with bridge inverter cells
implemented with IGBT modules [14], [15], or dual pack of
IGBTs [16], [17] as redundant unit. In the aforementioned
research, the important point is that the fault detection in the
redundant units is performed during the steady stat

4], [15], or dual pack of
IGBTs [16], [17] as redundant unit. In the aforementioned
research, the important point is that the fault detection in the
redundant units is performed during the steady state and not
during the transient state (IGBT switching). A very early fault
detection can be possible during switching in order to avoid
a fast fault propagation by short circuit in the complementary
power device of the same inverter pole; it has to be avoided in
less than 10 μs.
Most inverter drives use IGBTs as the switching device for
the following reasons: 1) The IGBT is available in high-voltage
and high-current ratings; 2) the IGBT’s ability to handle short-
circuit current up to 10 μs plays a critical role for increasing
the reliability of motor drive circuits; and 3) the easy control
of gate-signal commutations. Generally, the IGBTs are robust,
although they suffer failures due to excess of electrical and
thermal stresses that are very common in many applications.
Thus, IGBTs are vulnerable to fail, and it is estimated that
around 38% of the fault in industrial IM drives are due to
failure of the power device [18], [19]. A problem detected in the
literature is that the fault loca

nerable to fail, and it is estimated that
around 38% of the fault in industrial IM drives are due to
failure of the power device [18], [19]. A problem detected in the
literature is that the fault localization only identiﬁes the inverter
pole or cell damage instead of the power device affected [1],
[14], which is of crucial importance for the fault-tolerant motor
drive or neutral-point-clamped inverter [16] made with a single-
pack module of IGBTs, such as a redundant unit instead of a
complete inverter leg. The main interest of these tolerant con-
verter types appears because the manufacturer tends to package
high power device as a single-module type. In most of the
literature reported about the power-device location, the delay
time reported is not considered or is above 1 ms, as in [18] and
[19]. On the other hand, in [4] a detection time of less than 10 μs
is reported by using a ﬁeld-programmable gate array (FPGA),
but the delay times of the voltage sensor, optical couples,
analog data acquisition, and analog-to-digital converter are not
considered because the detection circuit is implemented outside
the gate driver, and in [12], a fuzzy-based approach for the
diagnosis of open c

ta acquisition, and analog-to-digital converter are not
considered because the detection circuit is implemented outside
the gate driver, and in [12], a fuzzy-based approach for the
diagnosis of open circuit of the device by using also the current-
trajectory approach is presented, but the implementation is not
in real time. Likewise, in [20], an algorithm of fault detection in
a pulsewidth modulation (PWM) inverter based on the current
trajectory and fuzzy signatures is implemented in an embedded
system by using a digital signal processor (DSP) and an FPGA
in order to share the tasks of control and diagnosis, but the
short-circuit failure in the device is not carried out; therefore,
both techniques are not suitable for a tolerant voltage-source-
inverter system because this failure type is very common.
In the literature, various approaches have been proposed to
detect IGBT failures by short circuit based on the measurement
of the collector voltage, the collector current, the gate voltage,
or the induced voltage across the emitter stray inductance [21]–
[25]. However, the implementation of fault-detection circuits
has higher cost, larger conﬁguration, and lower performance at
transi

ge,
or the induced voltage across the emitter stray inductance [21]–
[25]. However, the implementation of fault-detection circuits
has higher cost, larger conﬁguration, and lower performance at
transient, e.g., in [25], a short-circuit detecting scheme using
the gate-voltage pattern at turn-on switching of IGBT was
proposed; the problem is that the typical induced interference
in the gate signal at turn-on transient of IGBT and experimental
tests has not been considered.
In this paper, a new technique and experimental results of an
analog circuit implemented in the gate driver to detect failure
by open circuit and short circuit in the IGBT are presented.
The proposal is based on the gate-voltage behavior at turn-on
transient of the IGBTs of an IM drive.
II. FAULT DETECTION IN POWER DEVICE
FOR MOTOR DRIVE
In the literature, several techniques used have been reported
to detect failures in the inverter of an IM drive system. Failures
by short-circuit and open-circuit devices can be detected based
on mathematical transforms such as the fast Fourier transform
[26], discrete Fourier transform [27], and Park transform [28]
The main advantage of these techniques is that they may locate
the

ed based
on mathematical transforms such as the fast Fourier transform
[26], discrete Fourier transform [27], and Park transform [28]
The main advantage of these techniques is that they may locate
the damaged inverter leg by only analyzing the measured vari-
able. The problem of these techniques is the very long detection
delay time that is not suitable for avoiding a short-circuit fault
that may spread immediately to the complementary power
device of the same inverter leg in a failure-tolerant system.
Other techniques used to detect faults in power devices are
based on the voltage-level change, such as in the control signal
[17], normalized current deviation [20], [29], and voltage slope
[5], [13]. These techniques are faster than the previously men-
tioned techniques with regard to the delay time. However, the
fault location requires too many measurements. In summary,
fault-detection techniques in power devices applied to motor
drive can be classiﬁed as follows: 1) based on the analysis of the
current-vector trajectory and the instantaneous frequency [14];
2) based on ﬁeld-oriented composition of the voltage harmonics
at the switching frequency [15], and 3) based on the inherent

sis of the
current-vector trajectory and the instantaneous frequency [14];
2) based on ﬁeld-oriented composition of the voltage harmonics
at the switching frequency [15], and 3) based on the inherent
characteristic of continuous PWM [17]. The last technique is
more useful to be considered because the delay time for fault
detection is reduced. Nevertheless, the detection is not achieved
in an early stage because all analysis is taken in steady state. In
addition, the detection in the power device is limited to open-
circuit and inverter-leg failures, respectively.
When a failure occurs in the power device of the IM drive,
it is possible to maintain a no-degraded constant operation;
therefore, a fault-tolerant mechanism is necessary in order
to replace the damaged element in the material redundancy
framework.
A. Material Redundancy
In the literature, two material-redundancy types in the digital-
system area have been published [30] and retaken in the control-
system area [31]. These material redundancies are static and
dynamic. In both cases, a standby unit is used to replace the
damaged element, but in the static material redundancy, it is
not applied to a power system because the e

rial redundancies are static and
dynamic. In both cases, a standby unit is used to replace the
damaged element, but in the static material redundancy, it is
not applied to a power system because the energy manipulated
in this system type is large. In this way, the dynamic material
redundancy uses fewer modules at the cost of more information
processing and, consequently, at the cost of more response time.
Also, in this redundancy type, a module is usually working in


[Page 3]
RODRÍGUEZ-BLANCO et al.: FAILURE-DETECTION STRATEGY FOR IGBT BASED ON GATE-VOLTAGE BEHAVIOR
1627
Fig. 1.
Fault-tolerant schemes with dynamic material redundancy.
continuous operation, and when a fault occurs, then the backup
unit takes over. This requires fault detection to be observed if
the operational modules become faulty. After the fault detection
and isolation (diagnosis), the reconﬁguration task is to isolate
electrically and replace the damaged element, as shown in
Fig. 1.
The dynamic redundancy has found a special application in
the fault-tolerant electric systems due to its modular ﬂexibility,
as in the case of three-phase IM drive where the fault of power-
electronic device can be tolerated without

a special application in
the fault-tolerant electric systems due to its modular ﬂexibility,
as in the case of three-phase IM drive where the fault of power-
electronic device can be tolerated without affecting the control
algorithm.
B. Motor Drive With Material Redundancy
Most of the fault-tolerant schemes reported in the literature
are focused on the analysis of the IM drive. They use high-speed
fuses for the electrical-isolation stage [6]–[10], and also, some
of them use fault-detection techniques based on the switching
waveform of the power-electronic device [7], [8]. In both cases,
the most suitable replacement time to reduce the tracking
problem of the motor current has not been analyzed; in addition,
the replacement of damaged element is done by an auxiliary
pole instead of a single IGBT module. In this paper, a fault-
tolerant IM drive scheme reported in [11] and shown in Fig. 2
is used to emphasize the fault-detection technique proposed.
The fault-tolerant IM drive system of Fig. 2 is composed
of a three-phase inverter based on eight single IGBT modules.
The system designed is enabled to tolerate two IGBT faults,
located in a different position of the inverter, by means of

ig. 2 is composed
of a three-phase inverter based on eight single IGBT modules.
The system designed is enabled to tolerate two IGBT faults,
located in a different position of the inverter, by means of bidi-
rectional switches S(a,b,c) and S(a,b,c)(p,n) in order to replace
the damaged element. Therefore, any IGBT devices Q(a,b,c)p
and Q(a,b,c)n can be replaced by the material redundancy Qp
and Qn, respectively, e.g., when Qcn fails, then Qn is the
replacement device; Sc is the switch for isolation, and Scn is
the switch for connection.
The detection and the location of the fault are the ﬁrst and the
most important events in a tolerant-system sequence, as shown
in Fig. 3. After that, the tolerant mechanism is activated in order
to guarantee process-operation continuity of the motor drive
system used; the electrical isolation and the replacement of the
damaged element are made possible by the activation of the
isolation switches S(a,b,c) and connection switches S(a,b,c)(p,n).
The fault detection and the identiﬁcation of the damage
element are the events to achieve as soon as possible in a fault-
tolerant sequence. In this way, in this paper, a novel early fault-
detection technique is

tection and the identiﬁcation of the damage
element are the events to achieve as soon as possible in a fault-
tolerant sequence. In this way, in this paper, a novel early fault-
detection technique is presented, which is able to detect a fault
Fig. 2.
Fault-tolerant scheme for three-phase IM drive.
Fig. 3.
Fault-tolerant sequence for an IM drive system.
by short circuit and open circuit in the power devices of the
inverter. The technique proposed is achieved by monitoring the
gate signal of an IGBT, which is presented in the following
section.
III. FAULT DETECTION IN THE IGBT BASED ON THE
GATE-SIGNAL MEASUREMENT
A. Behavior of IGBT Gate-Voltage Signal
The analysis of this technique is derived from the equivalent
circuit seen from the gate to source of the IGBT. The region
from the gate to drain of the IGBT is modeled by CGD, which is
the most vulnerable region to fail because there is a great mobil-
ity of carrier in the channel during the IGBT turn-on transient.
This region is formed by variable capacitance CGDJ, which
models the depletion zone, and a ﬁxed capacitance COXD,
which models the oxide zone [32]. Fig. 4 shows a simpliﬁed
equivalent circuit that is seen from the gate to

by variable capacitance CGDJ, which
models the depletion zone, and a ﬁxed capacitance COXD,
which models the oxide zone [32]. Fig. 4 shows a simpliﬁed
equivalent circuit that is seen from the gate to emitter obtained
without taking into account the small modulation voltage of
the base resistor and the small turn-on voltage of the diode of
the internal bipolar junction transistor of the IGBT. Then, we
assumed that the drain D and collector C terminals are equal.
Therefore, the collector-to-emitter voltage VCE and the drain-
to-source voltage VDS of the IGBT are considered equivalent.

In the previous expression, it can be noted that IG is directly

the IGBT gate voltage. The changes of the IGBT gate are
characterized by three phases due to capacitance variations, and
it is shown in Fig. 5 for a speciﬁc experiment of a chopper
circuit when a constant gate current IG is forced to circulate
during the IGBT turn on to have a better deﬁnition of the
aforementioned behavior phases.
The behavior during the turn on of the IGBT depends on the
VGS and VDS voltage level

IG is forced to circulate

The behavior during the turn on of the IGBT depends on the
VGS and VDS voltage levels following different phases.
Phase 1 (t1 < t < t2): At this point, VDS(t) is greater; then,
the equivalent capacitance that it is seen from the gate to
emitter of the IGBT only depends on CGS because at this
point, CGDJ is smaller than COXD, and therefore, CGD is
omitted to simplify the analysis.
Phase 2 (t2 < t < t3): This phase has the most complex be-
havior because at this point, a plateau zone in the gate
signal is generated by the Miller effect causing the ﬁrst and
third terms of IG(t) in (3) to be neglected by simplicity;

Phase 3 (t3 < t < t4): During this phase and according to (2),

to (2),
the value of CGD can be considered equal to COXD be-
cause VDS(t) acquires a smaller value than VGS(t) during
IGBT turn-on; then, the total equivalent capacitance that
is seen from the gate to emitter is the electrical parallel of
COXD and CGS.
As has been mentioned previously, one of the electrical
parameters most affected during degradation is the capacitance
CGDJ. Therefore, it is possible to use the modulation of the
depletion zone in order to detect faults by short circuit or open
circuit in the IGBT by means of the Phase 2 measurement. The
basic behavior to simulate short circuit and open circuit is to
increase and to decrease CGDJ, respectively. This behavior can
be shown in the parametric analysis shown in Fig. 6, which was
implemented in PSpice software, where the capacitance CGDJ
is modiﬁed indirectly by means of AGD variation.
In Fig. 6, the fault zone is shown during IGBT turn on; the
fault can be evaluated from VT (threshold voltage) to upper
gate voltage, which can assure full evaluation of the depletion

the VT parameter is approximately a quarter of the gate–emitter

±20 V, and the plateau zone of the gate voltage is almost

the existence of a depletion region (Phase 2). Additionally, the
Phase 2 behavior can also be used to avoid physical destruction
of the IGBT and to activate quickly the tolerant mechanisms
in fast dynamic systems, such as the IM drive. Fig. 7 shows an
experimental test obtained in the IGBT gate signal for fault-free
case and faulty case by short circuit caused by overtemperature
and limited to nominal current in order to avoid fault propaga-
tion. The signal obtained for the faulty case during short-circuit
failure can be generalized for whatever application since the
CGDJ is reduced when AGD and/or εsi are decreasing, which
norm

propaga-
tion. The signal obtained for the faulty case during short-circuit
failure can be generalized for whatever application since the
CGDJ is reduced when AGD and/or εsi are decreasing, which
normally occur when there are thermal stress in the IGBT [35].
In a real-time application, the inductive interference in the
IGBT gate-voltage signal is a great problem, but it is well
known that this problem is mainly generated by the emitter
inductance Le. Therefore, it can then be solved by character-
izing the parasitic elements in order to consider them in the
fault-detection circuit design based on a high-speed differential
ampliﬁer with high input impedance.
The IGBT type used for the experimental circuit is the dis-
crete device GT15Q101 with high-voltage switching (1200 V),
low collector current (15 A), and TO-247 package. Experimen-
tal results in Fig. 7 show the validity of the IGBT destructive
analysis proposed for failure detection by using the gate-voltage
signal.
B. Fault-Detection Circuit Proposed
The detection-circuit design is based on the measurement
of the gate signal. The principle of the design consists of
measuring the energy of the IGBT gate charge during the turn
o

ion Circuit Proposed
The detection-circuit design is based on the measurement
of the gate signal. The principle of the design consists of
measuring the energy of the IGBT gate charge during the turn
on, from VT up to VT + 5 V, giving an output signal P1,
which is evaluated by using detection thresholds V Z1, V Z2,
and V Z3 in order to determine the IGBT short circuit or open
circuit. Fig. 8 shows detection windows V 1, V 2, and V 3 for
hysteresis zones of the fault-free and faulty cases.
Fig. 8.
Time diagram to determine the P1 width and P2 amplitude.
Fig. 9.
Fault-detection scheme in the IGBT based on the measurement and
evaluation in the turn-on transient period.
Fig. 9 shows the block diagram of the proposed detection
circuit to evaluate the gate energy of the IGBT.
The P1 pulsewidth is determined by a window detector,
which is composed of a comparator circuit with hysteresis. This
is capable of neglecting the turn-off phase, and thus, the limit
of the detection window is established at the turn-on phase by
means of two level comparators C1 and C2, which are tuned
from the threshold voltage VT to VT + 5 V in such a way that
the width of P1 may be proportional to the dynamics of

d at the turn-on phase by
means of two level comparators C1 and C2, which are tuned
from the threshold voltage VT to VT + 5 V in such a way that
the width of P1 may be proportional to the dynamics of the
Phase 2 of gate signal V ∗
GE of the IGBT. Note that V ∗
GE is the
IGBT gate voltage but without the induced interference caused
by the emitter inductance Le.
During the turn-on time of P1, the charge capacitor C is
pumped by a constant current source I; then, switch S1 is
enabled, and a sawtooth wave is produced (P2), and during
the turn off of P1, capacitor C is short circuited in order to
discharge it to 0 V. Therefore, the magnitude of the voltage
reached is proportional to the width of P1.
Then, the P2 voltage value is compared with three detection
threshold levels V Z1, V Z2, and V Z3, located around VT ,
VT + 5 V, and Vsat, but in fact, the tracking is performed by
the varying of C and Ik values. This comparison generates the
Z1, Z2, and Z3 signals. Finally, these signals are evaluated
at the end of P1 by the decision circuit which determines the

qualitative diagnostic of the system (failure-free or short-circuit
or open-circuit device or initial condition).
The diagnostic matrix is shown in Fig. 10 and is very simple;
thus, the circuit of decision is implemented by using a gate-
logic array.
It is well known that the induced interference on the IGBT
gate signal in a typical application can be a critical problem
during the measurement. This is due to the emitter inductor
Le = Le1 + Le2, where Le1 denotes the internal inductance of
the device and Le2 denotes the external inductance from the
emitter to ground. Thus, the problem can be solved characteriz-
ing this parasitic inductance and tuning the V ∗
GE measurement
circuit, which is formed by two instrumentation ampliﬁers and
a common differential ampliﬁer. Fig. 11 shows the detail of the
measurement circuit of V ∗
GE.

a common differential ampliﬁer. Fig. 11 shows the detail of the
measurement circuit of V ∗
GE.
The operation of the measurement circuit of V ∗
GE is based on
deducing the gate voltage VG without taking into account the
induced interference on the Phase 2 of the gate charge which
is produced by Le. Therefore, the VLe2 ampliﬁed K times is
reduced to VGE by means of U2, and K is obtained based on the
voltage relation of VLe1 and VLe2 of the IGBT and the power-
circuit parasitic inductances according to

An important aspect is that the K value can change for differ-

On the other hand, for a better evaluation of the gate signal,
the tu

On the other hand, for a better evaluation of the gate signal,
the turn-on transient period can be ampliﬁed by increasing the
value of the external gate resistance RG in order to have a
slow pumped of the gate but sacriﬁcing the maximum operating
frequency of the converter. The compromise is to establish the
turn-on transient period to three times greater than the total
fault-detection time. In this case, the delay time of the fault-
detection circuit proposed is 2.065 μs, which is not advisable
for high frequency, but this technique becomes very interesting
for medium power, and particularly for high-power motor-drive
applications, since the commutation frequency for medium
power is less than 25 kHz, and for high power, it is around
6 kHz.
IV. EXPERIMENTAL RESULTS
In order to verify the performance of the fault-detection
circuit proposed, an experimental setup of the fault-tolerant
IM drive system has been developed (see Fig. 12), which is
used to validate the novel detection technique proposed. The
experimental setup is composed of a three-phase IM of

etup of the fault-tolerant
IM drive system has been developed (see Fig. 12), which is
used to validate the novel detection technique proposed. The
experimental setup is composed of a three-phase IM of 1 hp,
an inverter with six IGBTs, a dc-link voltage ﬁxed at 300 V,
and an open-loop control Volts/Hertz generated by a DSP
(TMS320F2812) with a switching frequency imposed at 10 kHz
using a sinusoidal PWM technique; the bidirectional switches
for connection and isolation are formed by the back-to-back
IGBT conﬁguration, and the tolerant decision circuit has been

implemented by an integer circuit programmable logic device

(G4PH30K). The operating condition is set up by a collector

fa

iscrete type IGBT
(G4PH30K). The operating condition is set up by a collector
current of IC = 10 A, and the test collector–emitter voltage
VCE = 300 V. The failure-free and faulty conditions of the
fault-detection circuit are emulated by reducing and extend-

Experimental results under short-circuit failure.
resistance value RG signiﬁcantly, e.g., the failure by short-

the failure by open-circuit device is obtained by incrementing

The diagnosis matrix of Fig. 10(b) is easily evaluated if the
Z1, Z2, and Z3 signals are maintained and evaluated after
the P1 pulse because at this moment, the detection window is
ﬁnished. Therefore early fault detection is obtained.
The most important issue of this technique is the reduction
of time for fault detection. This is very important in a failure-
tolerant IM drive based on the material-redundancy approach
because it permits activating correctly the replacement mech-
anism of the damaged element in order to reduce the speed-
tracking e

failure-
tolerant IM drive based on the material-redundancy approach
because it permits activating correctly the replacement mech-
anism of the damaged element in order to reduce the speed-
tracking error during the fault transient and also to avoid the
failure, particularly for the short-circuit device, which can be
propagated to other power devices on the same leg of the
inverter. Therefore, the fault-detection mechanism must be
activated in less than 10 μs.
Fig. 14 shows the experimental results for current waveform
applying the detection technique proposed for a failure by
short circuit. The experimental conditions of the failure are
as follows: Qcn fails by short circuit, the electrical isolation
estimated is 5 ms after the fault detection, and replacement
time of the damaged element is at the next zero crossing of the
reference current of the motor.
When a short circuit appears in an inverter, the collector
current grows up to dangerous values, and although the IGBT
supports large transient currents that are not longer that 10 μs,
the current can exceed that time and ﬁnally destroy it. There-
fore, early detection is very important because it not only
protects the device unde

e transient currents that are not longer that 10 μs,
the current can exceed that time and ﬁnally destroy it. There-
fore, early detection is very important because it not only
protects the device under test and the complementary device of
the same leg by disabling the gate signal but also continues with
the tolerant sequence. This is accomplished by anticipating the
execution of the electrical-isolation stage whose delay time is
mainly attributed to the response time of the fuses used, which
is reﬂected in a smaller tracking error of the motor current, as
long as the replacement of the damaged element is executed at


[Page 8]
1632
IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON INDUSTRIAL ELECTRONICS, VOL. 58, NO. 5, MAY 2011
a more suitable time. That is because replacing the damaged
element in a speed-tracking control system as soon as possible
is not always the best decision because the control-loop type
determines the best moment to replace the damaged element. In
[12], for an open-loop or closed-loop control with low dynamic
response, such as volts/hertz strategy, the most suitable time
to replace the power device is at zero crossing of the motor
current, whereas for a closed-loop control with fast dyn

ol with low dynamic
response, such as volts/hertz strategy, the most suitable time
to replace the power device is at zero crossing of the motor
current, whereas for a closed-loop control with fast dynamic
response, such as ﬁeld-oriented strategy, the most suitable time
of replacement is as soon as possible because the time constant
of the controller is not strongly determined by the magnetic
ﬂux of the motor, which has a long time constant.
V. CONCLUSION
A fault-detection circuit based on the IGBT gate-voltage
behavior has been proposed, and in addition, this fault-detection
circuit has been implemented in the gate drive focused on a mo-
tor drive. The delay time of the fault-detection circuit obtained
is less than 3 μs; thus, this strategy is suitable for fault-tolerant
motor drive since the nonlinearities of the inverter as death
time, induced interference, and voltage peaks do not affect the
fault-free operation of the fault-detection circuit in the inverter.
A disadvantage of this strategy is that it is not generalized
for every power device, although the proposed fault-detection
circuit can also be applied with MOSFET because the current
of the channel MOS in the gate voltage

strategy is that it is not generalized
for every power device, although the proposed fault-detection
circuit can also be applied with MOSFET because the current
of the channel MOS in the gate voltage is similar. Another
disadvantage is that the measurement of VLe2 that attenuates the
induced interference adds an extra voltage sensor to the strategy
proposed.
